title: Data-Driven Evaluation of Training Action Space for Reinforcement Learning

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## DATA-DRIVEN EVALUATION OF TRAINING ACTION SPACE FOR REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Training action space selection for reinforcement learning (RL) is conflict-prone due to complex state-action relationships. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a Shapley-inspired methodology for training action space categorization and ranking. To reduce exponential-time shapley computations, the methodology includes a Monte Carlo simulation to avoid unnecessary explorations. The effectiveness of the methodology is illustrated using a cloud infrastructure resource tuning case study. It reduces the search space by 80% and categorizes the training action sets into dispensable and indispensable groups. Additionally, it ranks different training actions to facilitate high-performance yet cost-efficient RL model design. The proposed data-driven methodology is extensible to different domains, use cases, and reinforcement learning algorithms.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A reinforcement learning (RL) agent learns how to map states to actions in order to maximize a long-term cumulative award signal in a given environment. Figure 1 shows the various artifacts of an RL algorithm. An RL problem is defined by a quartet of ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ), where _S_ is a set of states or the state space; _A_ is a set of actions or the action space available to influence _S_ ; _Pa_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) = _Pr_ ( _st_ +1 = _s[′] |st_ = _s, at_ = _a_ ) is the transition probability which is the probability that action _a_ in state _s_ at time _t_ will lead to state _s[′]_ at time _t_ + 1; and finally, _Ra_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) is the immediate reward signal received after transitioning from state _s_ to state _s[′]_ , due to action _a_ . An RL agent training involves recognizing an optimal policy function, _π[∗]_ : _a ← s_ , from a corpus of _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_ to maximize the long-term cumulative reward,[�] _Ra_ . The reward function, _Ra_ , is defined ab initio for an efficient goal accomplishment. The transition probability or state-action mapping is defined by the environment dynamics. In many real-life use cases, the agent cannot directly sense the effect of its actions on the environment. This is particularly true when the state-action relationship cannot be modeled by either closed-form analytical expressions [26, 29, 58] or explicit rules as in the popular games such as Chess [28], Go [47, 46], and Atari [34]. This challenge is well documented in the literature [51, 57]. To address this challenge for RL model training [52, 33], simulation-based action models [50] play a pivotal role. The optimal choice of simulation parameters such as the training action space is a non-trivial challenge because of the curse of dimensionality [17], non-linearity [11], and non-uniform action set [25].

Training data valuation [22, 10] and associated artisanal software engineering efforts constitute a large part of the machine learning (ML) life cycle (or MLOps). Yet, most research and development efforts [36] focus on algorithms and infrastructure. Production-grade MLOps needs to handle data lifecycle management (DLM) [40] challenges including: fairness and bias in labeled datasets [16], data quality [9], limitations of benchmarks [42], and reproducibility concerns [39]. For RL, the DLM challenges are further compounded due to complexities arising from non-linear state-action interactions, partiallyobservable processes, non-isometric action spaces [32], and strong domain specificity [21] of action models. With the recent emphasis on ML explainability, traditional supervised learning and deep learning research communities are actively working on systematic data-driven frameworks [14, 53] for training data valuation. We need equivalent frameworks for RL to streamline conflict-prone training action selection [8, 37, 18]. Depending on the agent-environment interactions, different training actions have different relative contributions to the RL agent performance. Some training actions are indispensable because of their unique positions in the parametric space. Other dispensable actions have different relative contributions to the reward function. _Remarkably, a high-cardinality_

_training action space in many cases leads to lower cumulative reward than a well-designed training action space._ To the best of authors’ knowledge, there is hardly any data-driven tool for the evaluation of training action space for RL. Such a tool leads to superior RL agent performance, lower model training and maintenance cost, and strong multi-disciplinary collaboration [35].

This paper proposes a shapley-inspired [54] algorithm to categorize and rank training action sets. It also assists in recognizing cut-off cardinality for the training action space to reduce unnecessary exploration and ensure polynomial time complexity. While Section 2 describes the algorithms for RL training action space evaluation, Section 3 illustrates the effectiveness of the algorithms in a specific case study. Section 4 discusses the relevant work. Finally, Section 5 presents a summary with possible future directions.

## 2 ALGORITHM

The proposed framework provides a shapley-inspired methodology [49] to efficiently filter out unnecessary training actions and discover a near-optimal action set. Figure 2 shows the framework consists of two algorithms and how these two algorithms interact with each other to design a nearoptimal RL model by training action space filtering and evaluation. The first algorithm computes cutoff cardinality for the training action space. The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimium number of action points needed in a training action set to enable high-fidelity predictions within an acceptable computational complexity and an error bound. The second algorithm takes action points only above the cut-off cardinality and categorize action points into two classes: _{dispensable, indispensable}_ and ranking dispensable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards. Indispensable action points are absolute essential action points for the RL training to achieve the desired goal. On the other hand, dispensable action points are non-essential action points that can be discarded. However, the RL agent might perform sub-optimally without a disposable action point. By ranking disposable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards, the relative contributions of different training action points are estimated.

## 2.1 CUT-OFF CARDINALITY COMPUTATION FOR TRAINING ACTION POINTS

RL deals with compilation of a quartet ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) and computation of an optimal policy, _π[∗]_ which maximizes the long-term cumulative reward. The search space for a shapley-inspired technique needs to span across the power set for the training action space which has exponential complexity with training action points. To circumvent this problem, the proposed algorithm, as discussed in Algorithm 1, uses Monte Carlo method to compute the cut-off cardinality. The inputs to this algorithm include an RL algorithm, the power set for the training action space, the corresponding state space,

the transition probability, an acceptable performance margin, and an acceptable number of iterations. The output is the cut-off cardinality. The algorithm uses an iterative procedure to train the RL agent with different numbers of training actions, starting from all available training actions to only one training action. If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations, then we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one. The cut-off cardinality signifies computational efficiency — a larger cut-off cadinality means lower computational overhead and vice versa.

**Algorithm 1** Monte Carlo method for cut-off cardinality computation for the training action space **Input:** The RL algorithm, _R_ , the power set for the training action space, _P_ ( _A_ ), the corresponding state space, _P_ ( _S|A_ ), transition probability _**P**_ , an acceptable performance margin, _ϵ_ , and an acceptable number of iterations, _n_ . **Output:** Cut-off cardinality, _|A|cutoff_ **for** _k ←|A|_ to 1 **do repeat** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) **if** the cumulative reward improves by _ϵ_ with _n_ iterations **then** _status ← success_ **else** _status ← failure_ **end if until** all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted. **if** all statuses for a given cardinality, _k_ , are _failures_ **then** _|A|cutoff ← k_ + 1 **end if end for**

## 2.2 CATEGORIZATION AND RANKING OF TRAINING ACTIONS

Algorithm 2 offers a principled procedure for training action selection. As for inputs, it takes the combinations of training state-action pairs above the cut-off cardinality, determined by Algorithm 1, the RL algorithm, the reward function, the threshold condition, and the acceptable number of iterations. As for outputs, it predicts a categorization vector which classifies training action points into two classes: { _dispensable_ , _indispensable_ } and ranks dispensable training actions based on the corresponding cumulative reward. At its core, Algorithm 2 determines whether the policy determined from the given state-action pairs achieves the threshold condition within the acceptable number of iterations. The algorithm uncovers the indispensability of some training action sets for a given RL task. A training action set is deemed to be indispensable: _iff_ upon removal of that training action

The dispensable action points are rank listed in _L_ based on the corresponding cumulative reward. Essentially, _L_ informs the performance impact of different disposable action points on the RL agent.

**Input:** All combinations of training state-action pairs upto cut-off cardinality: _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_ , the RL algorithm, _R_ , the reward function, the threshold condition, and the acceptable number of iterations. **Output:** Categorization vector, _V_ , which classifies training action points into two classes: { _dispensable_ , _indispensable_ }. ## 2.3 ACTION SHAPLEY

The proposed framework adopts Action Shapley which introduces a formulation for the problem of equitable training action valuation in reinforcement learning. Reinforcement learning has three key building blocks. The first building block is the training action space, _A_ , the corresponding state space _S_ , and a pre-assigned reward function, _Ra_ . The second building block is a reinforcement learning algorithm, _R_ , which is treated as a black box. It takes ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) and computes an optimal policy function, _π[∗]_ . The third building block is the performance measure or the cumulative sum of reward,[�] _Ra_ . The goal of the framework is to compute the valuation for each training action point, _φi_ ( _A, R,_[�] _Ra_ ) as a function of three building blocks. A shapley technique is useful for action valuation because it satisfies nullity, symmetry, and linearity [45]. From game theory, _φ_ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1:

where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant. We call _φi_ the Action Shapley value of action _ai_ . Equation 1 suggests Action Shapley computation is with exponential time complexity w.r.t. the training action points. This exponential computational complexity warrants the need for the cut-off cardinality discussed in Algorithm 1.

## 3 CASE STUDY

In this section, we design a resource tuning example (in the cloud) to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm for the training action space evaluation. We evaluate the performance of an MDP [20]-based RL agent, as shown in Figure 1. The state-action mapping and transition probability are modeled using time-series auto-regression [38] after a PCA-based dimensionality reduction [41] with time complexity _O_ ( _klogk_ ), where _k_ is the number of principal components. The choice of the algorithms is purely driven by the nature of the training dataset. A more complex non-linear dataset warrants more complex sequential models such as long short-term memory (LSTM) [44] or attention based models [30]. For action updates, WLOG, an RL-based PID controller [1, 13] is used. Similar to time-series modeling, action update can be conducted by other policy learning algorithms such as SARSA [19].

As shown in Table 1, the objective of the RL agent for this case study is to quickly reduce high CPU utilization below a pre-assigned threshold for a given workload. In most infrastructure/cloud resource tuning technologies [55], CPU utilization represents a key metric. Therefore, the state space for this RL case study consists of virtual machine CPU utilization (%) metrics and the action space is defined by the VM resource set points, i.e., (# of vCPUs, Memory Size (GB)). The RL agent uses AWS boto3 SDK [2] to manipulate actions and AWS CloudWatch [3] for state space monitoring. The reward is defined as the number of time steps required by the agent to accomplish the objective multiplied by _−_ 1. The negative reward per time step was meant to push the agent to accomplish the task as fast as possible.

The training data for this case study was generated internally [4] with an open source library, _stress_ (https://linux.die.net/man/1/stress). It uses a rectangular workload. The peak of the workload uses the stress command: _sudo stress –io 4 –vm 2 –vm-bytes 1024M –timeout 500s_ . Essentially, the peak is running 4 _I/O_ stressors and 2 VM workers spinning on malloc with 1024 MB per worker for 500 s. The simulated rectangular workload has a time period of 600 s: a high-stress phase of 500 s is followed by an inactive phase of 100 s. The RL training action space is spanned by the power set drawn from the five pairs of EC2 configuration set points as shown in Table 2. Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results. Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4. That amounts to 81.25% reduction in the search space. For each EC2 instance in the training action space, the corresponding state metrics, i.e., CPU utilization (%) are shown in Figure 3. The training data was collected for a 24 hour period with 1 minute sampling interval. Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.

With this set up, different RL models are developed with different training action sets and the corresponding rewards and ranks are noted in Table 4. Remarkably, a high cardinality training action set does not guarantee the best reward: the agent with all training actions, _<small t3a, medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , does not yield the highest reward. In fact, the action set with _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ yields the highest reward. This pattern could be attributed to the state-action interaction in this particular case study. First, the parametric distances

As shown in Table 4, the reward scores can indeed be used for ranking different training action sets leading to a data-driven approach for training action selection. As shown in Figure 5, _large t3a_ is an indispensable element in the training action space for the given RL agent. Without this training action, the RL agent fails to accomplish the goal of bringing the CPU utilization below the critical threshold. Similar observations can be made about _xlarge t3a_ and _2xlarge t3a_ .

.

## 4 RELATED WORK

Shapley value was postulated in a classic paper in game theory [15] and influences the field of economics significantly [43]. Various shapley-inspired techniques have been applied to model diverse problems including voting [12], resource allocation [48] and bargaining [27]. Recent years saw increased application of shapley-inspired methodologies in machine learning feature importance evaluation and training data valuation [14, 10]. To the best of our knowledge, Shapley value has not been used to quantify training action valuation in a reinforcement learning context. The application of shapley-inspired technique for reinforcement learning is a challenging problem. First, the relationship between state-action pairs and transition probabilities are expensive to model. Also, often the RL training action points are not uniformly positioned. Finally, we have purposefully focused on a small sample cloud infrastructure use case instead of cliched RL use cases [5]. That is because the problem of optimal action selection has relatively more expensive in the cloud infrastructure space where the state-action relationship is only partially observable.

## 5 CONCLUSION

This paper proposes a data-driven methodology for training action space evaluation for RL. The methodology offers a principled framework for training action space categorization and ranking within a finite computational time. It unleashes a strategy for superior model performance and lower modeling cost. Additionally, the proposed methodology is completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms. Therefore, it is a general-purpose methodology extensible to different domains including distributed computing, network traffic control, healthcare, automatic locomotion, building management system, and industrial controls, and different machine learning algorithms such as PCA, Autoencoder, ARIMA, LSTM, Transformer, PID, SARSA, DQN, and many others. For the next phase of the development for this data-agnostic methodology, we are planning to contribute a general RL design library to relevant open source projects [6, 5, 7].

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a novel application of Shapley values to evaluate and reduce training action spaces in reinforcement learning, motivated by practical concerns in cloud resource tuning. The idea of using cooperative game theory to rank and categorize actions is conceptually interesting and could offer a principled way to improve data efficiency in RL. The case study demonstrates a non-trivial reduction in action space and identifies meaningful patterns in action importance.

Overview issues:

- Misapplication of Shapley Values to Actions Without Proper Game Formulation: The paper claims to use 'Action Shapley' values (Equation 1) but does not properly define a cooperative game where actions are players contributing to a payoff. In standard Shapley value applications, the value function must be defined over subsets of players (here, actions), but the paper does not specify how a subset of actions defines a policy or yields a cumulative reward. The algorithm instead tests removal of individual actions from a fixed set, which corresponds to ablation, not Shapley value computation. This undermines the theoretical foundation of the method and misrepresents the results as game-theoretic valuations when they are heuristic rankings based on leave-one-out performance. The authors should either reformulate the method in proper game-theoretic terms or reframe it as an ablation study with a different theoretical motivation.

- Cut-off Cardinality Algorithm Lacks Termination Guarantee and Clear Objective: Algorithm 1 iterates from full action set down to one action, but the condition for setting cut-off cardinality—'if all statuses for a given cardinality are failures'—is ambiguous and potentially unachievable. Since smaller action sets are more likely to fail (lower capacity), the algorithm may never find a k where all subsets fail, leading to non-termination. Moreover, the goal of the algorithm is unclear: is it seeking the smallest set that still works, or the largest set below which performance collapses? The current formulation conflates computational efficiency with performance, but no explicit trade-off is optimized. The algorithm needs a well-defined objective (e.g., minimal sufficient set) and a sound termination condition.

- Action Categorization Depends on Arbitrary Reference Set: Algorithm 2 determines indispensability by removing one action from the full set and checking if the goal is still achievable. However, this depends entirely on the choice of the base action set. An action deemed 'indispensable' in a 5-action set might be redundant in a different 4-action combination. Table 4 shows that several 4-action sets fail, yet the method only tests removals from the full set. This means the categorization is not intrinsic to the action but contingent on the specific experimental setup. The paper should either test all possible subsets or acknowledge that indispensability is relative to the initial action space, not an absolute property.

- Single Domain Case Study Limits Generalizability Claims: The entire evaluation is based on a single cloud tuning task with only five discrete actions. The paper claims the method is 'extensible to different domains' and 'completely agnostic' of algorithms, but provides no evidence beyond this one example. Without testing on at least one additional environment—especially one with continuous or larger discrete action spaces—the claims of generality are unsubstantiated. The non-uniform action spacing and small scale may make the results specific to this setup. The authors should either moderate their claims or include a second domain to support extensibility.

- Monte Carlo Label Misused and Computational Complexity Mischaracterized: The paper refers to Algorithm 1 as a 'Monte Carlo method' but it performs a deterministic grid search over action set sizes, not random sampling. True Monte Carlo approximation of Shapley values would involve random sampling of subsets, which is absent. This mislabeling misleads readers about the nature of the approximation. Furthermore, while the paper claims polynomial complexity due to cut-off cardinality, the underlying Shapley computation (if done properly) would still require exponential subsets even for a fixed k, and the repeated RL training in loops over subsets is computationally heavy. The complexity analysis is therefore misleading and needs correction.

- Lack of Comparison to Baseline Reduction Methods: The paper presents the proposed method as effective but does not compare it to simple or standard baselines such as random action pruning, magnitude-based heuristics, or variance-based selection. Without such comparisons, it is impossible to judge whether the Shapley-inspired framework adds value over trivial alternatives. For instance, removing the smallest resource configuration (small t3a) might achieve similar performance gains without any complex valuation. The authors should include ablation studies or comparisons to establish the necessity of their approach.

- No demonstration of method on a second environment: The paper claims the methodology is general-purpose and extensible to diverse domains, yet only evaluates it on a single, small-scale cloud resource tuning task with five discrete actions. Readers will wonder whether the approach transfers to environments with larger or continuous action spaces, or different reward structures. To support the claim of generality, the paper should apply the method to at least one additional standard RL environment—such as a control task from Gym with continuous actions (e.g., Pendulum or HalfCheetah) or a discrete-action game—and report cut-off cardinality, action rankings, and performance comparisons. Without this, the extensibility claim remains speculative.

- Missing comparison to simple pruning baselines: The paper asserts the proposed method enables superior RL performance and cost efficiency but does not compare it to basic action space reduction strategies. A natural question is whether the complex Shapley-inspired ranking outperforms a heuristic like removing the smallest or least-used actions. For instance, in the case study, simply excluding 'small t3a' might yield similar gains. The paper should compare the performance of models trained on action sets pruned by the proposed method versus those pruned by random removal or magnitude-based rules (e.g., lowest resource capacity). This would demonstrate the added value of the framework beyond trivial alternatives.

- No computation of Action Shapley values as defined in Equation 1: Equation 1 defines Action Shapley values using a sum over all subsets, which is central to the theoretical motivation. However, the algorithms never compute this quantity, nor do they approximate it via subset sampling. Instead, the method relies on leave-one-out testing from a fixed set. This step requires justification because without computing or approximating the actual Shapley value, the connection to game-theoretic valuation is purely nominal. The authors should either compute approximate Shapley values for the case study using random subset sampling and compare them to their ranking, or reframe the method as an ablation-based heuristic and drop the Shapley framing.

- No analysis of sensitivity to training data distribution: The method is described as data-driven and agnostic to algorithms, but the case study uses a fixed, synthetic workload. Readers will wonder whether the action rankings and cut-off cardinality change under different workloads or data shifts. Since the paper emphasizes real-world applicability, it should test the stability of the results under varied data conditions—for example, by re-running the analysis on a non-rectangular or bursty workload and reporting whether the same actions are deemed indispensable. This would strengthen the claim that the method provides robust insights across operational conditions.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Reformulate or rename the 'Action Shapley' method to accurately reflect the ablation-based ranking, either by adopting proper game-theoretic definitions or reframing as a heuristic importance metric.

- Correct the description of Algorithm 1, clarify its objective, ensure termination, and remove the misused 'Monte Carlo' label unless random sampling is introduced.

- Conduct a more robust action categorization that accounts for dependencies on the reference set, or explicitly state that indispensability is context-dependent.

- Add at least one additional case study in a different domain or with a larger/continuous action space to support generalizability claims.

- Include comparisons to baseline action space reduction methods to demonstrate the added value of the proposed framework.

comments.title: Incorrect Shapley Value Formula and Arbitrary Constant
comments.quote:
From game theory, $\phi$ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1:

$\phi_{i}=C\sum_{S\subseteq A-\{i\}}\frac{\sum R_{a}(S\cup\{i\})-\sum R_{a}(S)}{\binom{n-1}{|S|}}$ (1)

where, the sum is over all subsets of $A$ not containing $i$ and $C$ is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation: The formula for Action Shapley in Equation 1 deviates from the standard definition. The denominator $\binom{n-1}{|S|}$ does not correspond to the correct combinatorial weighting in Shapley value computation, which should be $\frac{|S|!(n - |S| - 1)!}{n!}$ to reflect the number of permutations in which player $i$ joins coalition $S$. Using a binomial coefficient here distorts the marginal contribution weights. Moreover, introducing an arbitrary constant $C$ violates the uniqueness of the Shapley value, which is fully determined by the axioms of efficiency, symmetry, and additivity. Without fixing the formula and removing $C$, the expression does not compute a valid Shapley value.

comments.title: Value Function Undefined for Arbitrary Subsets
comments.quote: The goal of the framework is to compute the valuation for each training action point, $\phi_{i}(A,\mathcal{R},\sum R_{a})$ as a function of three building blocks. A shapley technique is useful for action valuation because it satisfies nullity, symmetry, and linearity *[45]*. From game theory, $\phi$ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1:
comments.explanation: The paper assumes that $\sum R_a(S)$ is well-defined for any subset $S \subseteq A$, but it does not specify how the RL algorithm $\mathcal{R}$ operates on restricted action spaces. Is the policy retrained from scratch using only actions in $S$? Is the reward estimated via simulation or importance sampling? Without a precise definition of the value function $v(S) = \sum R_a(S)$, the marginal contributions in Equation 1 remain ambiguous. This is not a minor implementation detail—it is foundational to any game-theoretic valuation. The method cannot claim Shapley inspiration without first defining how coalitions of actions induce performance outcomes.

comments.title: Misuse of 'Monte Carlo' in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: the proposed algorithm, as discussed in Algorithm 1, uses Monte Carlo method to compute the cut-off cardinality.
comments.explanation: The term 'Monte Carlo method' is incorrect here. A Monte Carlo approach would involve random sampling of action subsets to estimate properties like failure rate or expected reward. Instead, Algorithm 1 performs a deterministic sweep over all subsets of each cardinality, which is a combinatorial grid search. There is no random sampling, no convergence in probability, and no statistical estimation. Using 'Monte Carlo' misleads readers into believing the method has stochastic approximation properties it does not possess. The label should be replaced with 'iterative subset evaluation' or 'combinatorial search'.

comments.title: Cut-off Cardinality Algorithm Lacks Termination Guarantee
comments.quote:
if all statuses for a given cardinality,  $k$ , are failures then
$|A|_{cutoff} \gets k + 1$
comments.explanation: The algorithm assigns $|A|_{\text{cutoff}}$ when all subsets of size $k$ fail, but continues iterating to smaller $k$ without a break. This means the assignment can be overwritten at lower cardinalities, potentially resulting in an incorrect or inconsistent final value. Worse, if even one subset of size 1 succeeds, the condition never triggers and $|A|_{\text{cutoff}}$ remains undefined. The absence of a termination mechanism undermines the algorithm's correctness. It should either break after the first assignment or reverse the loop to find the minimal working cardinality.

comments.title: Indispensability Test Depends on Full Action Set
comments.quote:
A training action set is deemed to be indispensable: iff upon removal of that training action

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

point, the RL agent fails to accomplish the goal within a finite number of iterations *[24, 56, 23]*.
comments.explanation: The definition of indispensability is based solely on removing one action from the full set, but this makes the categorization contingent on the initial action space. An action deemed 'indispensable' in the full set might be redundant in a different combination. For example, Table 4 shows that several 4-action sets fail, yet the method only tests removals from the 5-action set. This means the label reflects necessity relative to a specific configuration, not an intrinsic property of the action. The paper should either test all subsets or reframe indispensability as context-dependent.

comments.title: Categorization Vector Indexed by Set, Not Action
comments.quote:
$\mathcal{V}(\bm{A}-a_{i})\leftarrow indispensable$
else
$\mathcal{V}(\bm{A}-a_{i})\leftarrow dispensable$
comments.explanation: The categorization vector $\mathcal{V}$ is intended to classify individual actions, but it is indexed by $\bm{A} - a_i$, which is a set. This creates a type mismatch: $\mathcal{V}$ should map $a_i$ to a label, not a subset. The current notation suggests the label applies to the entire reduced set, which contradicts the stated output of individual action categorization. The assignments should be $\mathcal{V}(a_i) \leftarrow \text{indispensable}$ to correctly associate the result with the action under test.

comments.title: Incorrect Justification for Search Space Reduction
comments.quote: Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results. Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4. That amounts to  $81.25\%$  reduction in the search space.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'below the cut-off cardinality of 4' incorrectly refers to subsets of size less than 4, but the paper analyzes the 6 subsets of size 4 and 5—the *top* of the power set. Subsets below cardinality 4 total 26 out of 32, so the remaining 6 are those *at or above* 4. The claimed 81.25% reduction is numerically correct ($1 - 6/32$), but the justification misrepresents the selection logic. The text should say 'at or above cardinality 4' to align with the actual analysis scope.

comments.title: Overstated Generality Beyond Reinforcement Learning
comments.quote: Additionally, the proposed methodology is completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms. Therefore, it is a general-purpose methodology extensible to different domains including distributed computing, network traffic control, healthcare, automatic locomotion, building management system, and industrial controls, and different machine learning algorithms such as PCA, Autoencoder, ARIMA, LSTM, Transformer, PID, SARSA, DQN, and many others.
comments.explanation: The claim of agnosticism and extensibility to models like PCA, ARIMA, or Autoencoder is unfounded. These are not reinforcement learning methods and do not operate on action spaces or interact with environments via policies. The proposed algorithms require inputs such as transition dynamics, reward functions, and an RL training loop—components absent in unsupervised or forecasting models. Including them under the method's scope misrepresents its applicability. The extensibility claim should be limited to RL and sequential decision-making frameworks.

comments.title: Ambiguous Baseline for Reward Improvement
comments.quote: if the cumulative reward improves by  $\epsilon$  with  $n$  iterations then
comments.explanation: The condition checks for improvement by $\epsilon$, but does not specify relative to what baseline. Is it improvement over the previous iteration, over a random policy, or over the best policy at $k+1$ actions? Without a fixed reference, the comparison is meaningless—for example, independent training from scratch makes 'improvement' ill-defined. The algorithm must compare against a consistent baseline, such as the best policy from the next higher cardinality, to measure degradation meaningfully.

comments.title: Unjustified Claim of Polynomial Time Complexity
comments.quote: It also assists in recognizing cut-off cardinality for the training action space to reduce unnecessary exploration and ensure polynomial time complexity.
comments.explanation: The claim that the method 'ensures polynomial time complexity' is incorrect. Algorithm 1 involves training an RL agent for multiple subsets, and RL training itself is typically superlinear in time and sample complexity. Even if only one subset per cardinality is tested, repeated full training runs prevent the overall process from being polynomial. The phrase overstates efficiency and contradicts standard complexity analysis. The method reduces search space but does not ensure polynomial runtime—this should be revised or removed.

comments.title: Misattribution of Reference [5] to RL Use Cases
comments.quote: Finally, we have purposefully focused on a small sample cloud infrastructure use case instead of cliched RL use cases [5].
comments.explanation: Reference [5] is Thomas & Brunskill (ICML 2016), a foundational paper on off-policy evaluation, not a benchmark environment or 'cliched RL use case'. The citation is therefore a misattribution—it does not support the claim about standard domains like Atari or MuJoCo. This error suggests a lack of engagement with the cited literature and undermines scholarly rigor. The reference must be corrected or replaced with a relevant source.

comments.title: Undefined Step-Size Selection Algorithm
comments.quote: Using Algorithm ??, the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0.1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The reference 'Algorithm ??' is undefined—no algorithm is presented for step-size selection. The value 0.1 appears without justification or methodological description. This creates a gap in reproducibility and weakens the data-driven claim. The paper must either define the algorithm (e.g., grid search, line search) or state the step size as a fixed hyperparameter. Placeholder citations cannot stand in a scientific contribution.

comments.title: Incorrect Parametric Boundary Includes Infeasible Point
comments.quote: The recommended points are superimposed on the training action parameter space to illustrate their relative position with respect to the parametric boundary which is defined by the trapezium with vertices:  $[(2,2), (2,8), (8,32), (8,2)]$  in the (# of vCPUs, Memory Size (GB)) space.
comments.explanation: The vertex (8,2) is not a valid configuration in the action space—no EC2 instance has 8 vCPUs and 2 GB memory. The smallest memory for 8 vCPUs is 32 GB (2xlarge t3a). Including (8,2) in the boundary misrepresents the feasible region. The convex hull of the actual points would not contain this point. The boundary should reflect either the actual configurations or a justified design space, not an infeasible trapezium.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Ranking of Dispensable Actions
comments.quote: Rank List, $\mathcal{L}\leftarrow$ dispensable actions sorted in order of the corresponding cumulative rewards
comments.explanation: It is unclear which reward value corresponds to each action. Is it the cumulative reward achieved when $a_i$ is removed? If so, higher rewards mean less degradation, so top-ranked actions are less important—but this contradicts the intent of ranking by 'relative contribution'. The directionality of the ranking is ambiguous: does a higher rank mean greater importance or better residual performance? The paper must specify whether the sort is by reward with removal (descending) or by reward drop (descending), and clarify the interpretation.

comments.title: Input Includes Ill-Defined State Space Notation
comments.quote: the corresponding state space,  $\mathcal{P}(S|A)$
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathcal{P}(S|A)$ is malformed. $\mathcal{P}$ typically denotes a power set or probability distribution, and $S|A$ suggests conditioning, but state spaces are not defined as power sets conditioned on actions. The standard formulation uses a fixed state space $S$ with transition dynamics $P(s'|s,a)$. The input should list $S$ and $P$, not $\mathcal{P}(S|A)$, which has no clear interpretation and may confuse readers about state-action dependencies.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a Shapley-inspired methodology for evaluating and pruning training action spaces in reinforcement learning. While the concept of using Shapley values for action valuation is novel, the paper suffers from significant methodological gaps, insufficient theoretical grounding, and limited empirical validation. The algorithms lack proper theoretical analysis, the case study is too narrow to support broad claims, and critical computational complexity issues are inadequately addressed.

The paper addresses an important problem in RL - the systematic evaluation of training action spaces - which has practical relevance for reducing computational costs and improving performance. The core idea of applying Shapley values to action valuation is conceptually interesting and could fill a gap in the literature. However, the execution falls short on multiple fronts: the proposed algorithms lack rigorous theoretical justification, the computational complexity claims are unsubstantiated, and the single case study provides insufficient evidence for the method's generalizability.

Overview issues:

- Unsubstantiated Computational Complexity Claims: The paper claims to achieve polynomial time complexity through cut-off cardinality computation (Algorithm 1), but provides no theoretical analysis to support this. Algorithm 1 still requires evaluating all subsets of actions for each cardinality k, which remains exponential. The Monte Carlo approach mentioned in Section 2.1 is not integrated into the algorithm pseudocode, and it's unclear how it reduces complexity. Without formal analysis of the algorithm's time complexity or empirical scaling results, the polynomial time claim is unfounded. This matters because computational efficiency is a key contribution claim, and exponential Shapley computation is a known limitation in the field.

- Missing Theoretical Guarantees for Shapley Approximation: The paper introduces Action Shapley values (Equation 1) but provides no theoretical analysis of their properties or approximation quality when using the proposed algorithms. There is no discussion of bias, variance, or convergence guarantees for the Monte Carlo approach mentioned in Section 2.1. The relationship between the cut-off cardinality and the accuracy of Shapley value estimates is not established. This is critical because Shapley values have specific axiomatic properties (nullity, symmetry, linearity) that may not hold under approximation. Without theoretical grounding, readers cannot assess whether the proposed method preserves these desirable properties.

- Inadequate Baseline Comparisons: The case study in Section 3 lacks comparison with existing action space reduction methods mentioned in the literature (e.g., dynamic masking, relative reduction techniques). The paper only compares different action subsets within its own framework but does not evaluate against alternative approaches. This makes it impossible to assess whether the proposed Shapley-based method actually outperforms simpler or existing techniques. For a methodology paper, demonstrating superiority or complementarity to existing methods is essential for establishing its value to the field.

- Limited Generalizability Evidence: The paper claims the methodology is "completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms" and extensible to "different domains including distributed computing, network traffic control, healthcare, automatic locomotion" (Section 5). However, only a single cloud resource tuning case study is presented. The action space in this case study is tiny (5 discrete actions), which doesn't demonstrate scalability to larger or continuous action spaces. No experiments are shown with different RL algorithms, environments, or problem domains. The claim of generalizability is entirely unsupported by evidence, which is a significant concern for a paper proposing a general-purpose methodology.

- Algorithm Implementation Ambiguities: Algorithm 2 contains critical implementation details that are unclear. The condition "if Threshold condition is never satisfied within the acceptable number of iterations" is problematic because it requires running the RL agent to convergence for each action removal, which is computationally expensive. The algorithm doesn't specify how to handle stochastic environments where performance varies between runs. Additionally, the ranking of dispensable actions based on "corresponding cumulative rewards" is ambiguous - is this the reward when only that action is removed, or some other metric? These ambiguities make the algorithm difficult to implement and reproduce.

- Insufficient Ablation Study: The paper lacks proper ablation studies to demonstrate the contribution of each component. It's unclear how much the cut-off cardinality computation (Algorithm 1) contributes to the overall performance versus simply using all actions above some threshold. The impact of different values for parameters like ε (error margin) and n (acceptable iterations) is not analyzed. Without understanding how each component affects the results, readers cannot assess the method's robustness or which aspects are truly novel and impactful.

- No demonstration of non-vacuous results: The paper needs to show that its method can fail when it should fail. A complete evaluation would include a counterexample where the method incorrectly categorizes an indispensable action as dispensable, or where the cut-off cardinality is set too low, leading to poor performance. This matters because without such examples, readers cannot assess whether the method's success in the case study is due to the algorithm's effectiveness or specific properties of that environment. The paper should construct a synthetic environment where certain actions are known to be critical (e.g., actions that prevent the agent from reaching certain states) and demonstrate that the method correctly identifies them, while also showing scenarios where naive approaches would fail.

- Missing worked example for continuous action spaces: The paper claims the methodology is extensible to different domains but only demonstrates it on a tiny discrete action space (5 actions). A theory paper should include at least one concrete computation for a continuous action space, such as the classic cart-pole or inverted pendulum control problem. This would involve showing how the discretization is performed, how the cut-off cardinality is determined in a continuous setting, and computing the Action Shapley values for a sample of discretized actions. Without this, the claim of generalizability to continuous control problems remains unsubstantiated, which is significant since many real-world RL applications involve continuous actions.

- Underdeveloped implementation guidance: The paper provides high-level algorithms but lacks critical implementation details needed for reproducibility. Algorithm 2 requires running an RL agent to convergence for each action removal, but doesn't specify convergence criteria, how many episodes to run, or how to handle stochasticity. The Monte Carlo method mentioned for reducing Shapley computation complexity is not integrated into the pseudocode. The paper should provide concrete guidance on computational budget allocation, hyperparameter selection (ε and n values), and practical considerations for parallelization. Without these details, researchers cannot implement or reproduce the method, undermining its practical utility.

- Missing comparison with baseline action selection methods: The paper claims superior performance but never compares against established action space reduction techniques. A complete evaluation should include comparisons with at least two baseline methods: (1) random action subset selection of the same cardinality, and (2) domain knowledge-based action pruning (e.g., removing actions with low marginal utility estimated from pilot runs). The comparison should measure both computational efficiency and final policy performance. This matters because without baseline comparisons, it's impossible to determine whether the Shapley-based approach provides meaningful benefits over simpler alternatives, which is essential for justifying the method's computational overhead.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents an interesting concept but suffers from significant methodological gaps and insufficient empirical validation. The theoretical foundations are weak, computational complexity claims are unsubstantiated, and the single limited case study cannot support the broad generalizability claims. Substantial revisions including theoretical analysis, additional experiments across multiple domains, and proper baseline comparisons are needed.

Revision targets:

- Provide formal theoretical analysis of computational complexity, including proofs or bounds on the algorithm's runtime

- Add theoretical guarantees for the Shapley value approximation, including analysis of bias and variance

- Include comprehensive baseline comparisons with existing action space reduction methods

- Demonstrate the method on multiple diverse environments and RL algorithms to support generalizability claims

- Clarify algorithm implementation details and add ablation studies analyzing each component's contribution

comments.title: Algorithm 1 has critical loop overwrite issue
comments.quote:
for  $k \gets |A|$  to 1 do
repeat
$\pi^{*} \gets \mathcal{R}(S, A, P_{a}, R_{a})$
if the cumulative reward improves by  $\epsilon$  with  $n$  iterations then
status  $\leftarrow$  success
else
status  $\leftarrow$  failure
end if
until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted.
if all statuses for a given cardinality,  $k$ , are failures then
$|A|_{cutoff} \gets k + 1$
end if
end for
comments.explanation: The algorithm's loop structure has a critical flaw. The outer for loop continues iterating even after setting $|A|_{cutoff} \gets k + 1$. If cardinality k fails, the cutoff is set to k+1, but the algorithm proceeds to test k-1, k-2, etc. This makes the output meaningless as it doesn't represent the smallest cardinality that fails. The algorithm should terminate immediately after finding the first failing cardinality.

comments.title: Incorrect Shapley value formula
comments.quote: $\phi_{i}=C\sum_{S\subseteq A-\{i\}}\frac{\sum R_{a}(S\cup\{i\})-\sum R_{a}(S)}{\binom{n-1}{|S|}}$ (1)
comments.explanation: The Shapley value formula is mathematically incorrect. The standard Shapley value formula is $\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq A \setminus \{i\}} \frac{|S|!(n-|S|-1)!}{n!}[v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)]$, where $v(S)$ is the value function for coalition $S$. The paper's formula uses $\frac{1}{\binom{n-1}{|S|}}$ as the weighting factor, but the correct weight should be $\frac{|S|!(n-|S|-1)!}{n!} = \frac{1}{n \binom{n-1}{|S|}}$. The missing factor of $\frac{1}{n}$ means the formula doesn't satisfy the efficiency property of Shapley values.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 uses undefined variables in RL call
comments.quote: $\pi^{*} \gets \mathcal{R}(S, A, P_{a}, R_{a})$
comments.explanation: The RL algorithm call uses variables S and A that are not properly scoped. The inputs are power sets $\mathcal{P}(A)$ and $\mathcal{P}(S|A)$, but the algorithm never specifies which specific action set of cardinality k is being tested. The current pseudocode suggests using the full action space A for every iteration, which defeats the purpose of testing different cardinalities. The algorithm should iterate through action sets of size k from $\mathcal{P}(A)$ and pass each specific action set $A_k$ to the RL algorithm.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 lacks Monte Carlo sampling
comments.quote: To circumvent this problem, the proposed algorithm, as discussed in Algorithm 1, uses Monte Carlo method to compute the cut-off cardinality.
comments.explanation: The text claims Algorithm 1 uses Monte Carlo methods, but the pseudocode performs exhaustive enumeration ('until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted'). Monte Carlo methods involve random sampling to estimate quantities, not deterministic testing of all possibilities. This is a fundamental mismatch between the claimed methodology and the actual implementation. Either remove the Monte Carlo claim or modify the algorithm to use random sampling of action subsets.

comments.title: Incorrect RL problem definition notation
comments.quote: An RL problem is defined by a quartet of $(S,A,P_{a},R_{a})$, where $S$ is a set of states or the state space; $A$ is a set of actions or the action space available to influence $S$; $P_{a}(s,s^{\prime})=Pr(s_{t+1}=s^{\prime}|s_{t}=s,a_{t}=a)$ is the transition probability which is the probability that action $a$ in state $s$ at time $t$ will lead to state $s^{\prime}$ at time $t+1$; and finally, $R_{a}(s,s^{\prime})$ is the immediate reward signal received after transitioning from state $s$ to state $s^{\prime}$, due to action $a$.
comments.explanation: The standard MDP definition uses $(S,A,P,R,\gamma)$ where $P: S \times A \times S \rightarrow [0,1]$ and $R: S \times A \times S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. The notation $P_a(s,s')$ and $R_a(s,s')$ incorrectly suggests these are parameterized by a specific action $a$, when they should be functions of the action variable. The transition probability should be $P(s'|s,a)$ and the reward function $R(s,a,s')$.

comments.title: Incorrect policy function notation
comments.quote: An RL agent training involves recognizing an optimal policy function, $\pi^{*}:a\leftarrow s$, from a corpus of $\{\langle s_{i},a_{i}\rangle\}$ to maximize the long-term cumulative reward, $\sum R_{a}$.
comments.explanation: The policy function notation $\pi^{*}:a\leftarrow s$ is non-standard and confusing. Standard notation is $\pi^{*}: S \rightarrow A$ or $\pi^{*}(s) = a$. Additionally, the cumulative reward should be $\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t R(s_t,a_t,s_{t+1})]$ or similar, not $\sum R_a$ which is mathematically ambiguous. The notation $R_a$ was previously defined as a function of state transitions, not a summable quantity.

comments.title: Algorithm interaction description mismatch
comments.quote: The first algorithm computes cutoff cardinality for the training action space. The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimum number of action points needed in a training action set to enable high-fidelity predictions within an acceptable computational complexity and an error bound. The second algorithm takes action points only above the cut-off cardinality and categorize action points into two classes: {dispensable, indispensable} and ranking dispensable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards.
comments.explanation: There is a logical inconsistency. If Algorithm 1 computes the minimum number of action points needed (cut-off cardinality), then by definition all points above this threshold should be sufficient for high-fidelity predictions. Algorithm 2 then categorizing these pre-filtered points as dispensable contradicts the purpose of the cut-off. The framework needs clarification: either Algorithm 2 works on the full action space using cut-off as a constraint, or the categorization distinguishes between minimally sufficient versus truly essential actions.

comments.title: Undefined constant C in Shapley formula
comments.quote: where, the sum is over all subsets of $A$ not containing $i$ and $C$ is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation: The constant $C$ in the Shapley value formula is problematic. In standard Shapley value theory, there is no arbitrary constant - the formula is uniquely determined by the axioms. Adding an arbitrary constant $C$ would violate the efficiency axiom of Shapley values, which requires that the sum of all Shapley values equals the total value created by the grand coalition. The paper provides no justification for this constant.

comments.title: Ambiguous cumulative reward notation
comments.quote: The third building block is the performance measure or the cumulative sum of reward, $\sum R_{a}$.
comments.explanation: The notation $\sum R_{a}$ is mathematically ambiguous and inconsistent with its later usage in Equation 1. In the building block description, $\sum R_{a}$ appears to be a single scalar value representing total cumulative reward. However, in Equation 1, $\sum R_{a}(S)$ and $\sum R_{a}(S \cup \{i\})$ suggest that $\sum R_{a}$ is a function that takes an action set as input. The paper never defines this function formally.

comments.title: Performance improvement condition is ambiguous
comments.quote: if the cumulative reward improves by  $\epsilon$  with  $n$  iterations then
comments.explanation: The condition 'improves by ε with n iterations' is mathematically ambiguous. It could mean: (1) reward increases by ≥ε compared to previous iteration, (2) reward after n iterations is ε higher than initial reward, or (3) reward improves by ε in each of n iterations. This ambiguity prevents correct implementation. The condition needs precise mathematical definition.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 has undefined status variable scope
comments.quote: if all statuses for a given cardinality,  $k$ , are failures then
comments.explanation: The algorithm references 'all statuses for a given cardinality k' but never defines how statuses are aggregated across different action sets of the same cardinality. The inner repeat loop sets a single status variable, but this gets overwritten for each action set tested. There's no mechanism to track whether ALL action sets of cardinality k failed.

comments.title: Undefined notation in algorithm
comments.quote:
repeat
for $<s_{i},a_{i}>$ do
$\pi^{*}\leftarrow\mathcal{R}(S,A,P_{a},R_{a})$
if Threshold condition is $never$ satisfied $within$ the acceptable number of iterations then
$\mathcal{V}(\bm{A}-a_{i})\leftarrow indispensable$
else
$\mathcal{V}(\bm{A}-a_{i})\leftarrow dispensable$
end if
end for
comments.explanation: The algorithm uses undefined notation that prevents implementation. The variables $S$, $A$, $P_{a}$, and $R_{a}$ appear in the RL algorithm call $\mathcal{R}(S,A,P_{a},R_{a})$ but are not defined in the input specification or text. The notation $\bm{A}$ in $\mathcal{V}(\bm{A}-a_{i})$ is unclear - it could represent the full action set or current subset. The angle bracket notation $<s_{i},a_{i}>$ is non-standard without explanation.

comments.title: Logical error in indispensability condition
comments.quote:
sk. A training action set is deemed to be indispensable: iff upon removal of that training action

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

point, the RL agent fails to accomplish the goal within a finite number of iterations *[24, 56, 23]*. All other action points are dispensab
comments.explanation: There is a logical inconsistency between definition and implementation. The text defines indispensability based on 'failure to accomplish the goal within a finite number of iterations' (unbounded) but the algorithm checks 'Threshold condition is never satisfied within the acceptable number of iterations' (bounded). An action requiring 1000 iterations when the acceptable limit is 500 would be classified as dispensable by the algorithm but indispensable by the definition.

comments.title: Ambiguous cumulative reward calculation
comments.quote: Rank List, $\mathcal{L}\leftarrow$ dispensable actions sorted in order of the corresponding cumulative rewards
comments.explanation: The algorithm mentions sorting dispensable actions by 'corresponding cumulative rewards' but never specifies how these rewards are calculated. Is it the cumulative reward obtained during the threshold condition test? The total reward over all episodes? The reward difference between full and reduced action sets? Without this specification, the ranking criterion is undefined and cannot be implemented.

comments.title: Undefined termination condition
comments.quote: until all necessary training action sets are evaluated.
comments.explanation: The algorithm terminates when 'all necessary training action sets are evaluated' but never defines what makes a training action set 'necessary' versus 'unnecessary'. This creates a circular dependency - the algorithm determines indispensability but needs to know which sets are necessary to terminate. The algorithm could run indefinitely or stop prematurely depending on this undefined condition.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated computational time claim
comments.quote: The methodology offers a principled framework for training action space categorization and ranking within a finite computational time.
comments.explanation: The paper claims the methodology operates within 'finite computational time' but provides no theoretical analysis or empirical evidence to support this claim. Algorithm 1 still requires evaluating all subsets of actions for each cardinality k, which remains exponential in complexity. The Monte Carlo approach mentioned in Section 2.1 is not integrated into the algorithm pseudocode, and no formal complexity analysis is provided.

comments.title: Overstated generalizability claim
comments.quote: Additionally, the proposed methodology is completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms. Therefore, it is a general-purpose methodology extensible to different domains including distributed computing, network traffic control, healthcare, automatic locomotion, building management system, and industrial controls, and different machine learning algorithms such as PCA, Autoencoder, ARIMA, LSTM, Transformer, PID, SARSA, DQN, and many others.
comments.explanation: The claim that the methodology is 'completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms' and extensible to all listed domains and algorithms is not supported by evidence. The paper only demonstrates the method on a single cloud resource tuning case study with a tiny discrete action space (5 actions). No experiments are shown with different RL algorithms, environments, or problem domains.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated performance superiority claim
comments.quote: It unleashes a strategy for superior model performance and lower modeling cost.
comments.explanation: The paper claims 'superior model performance and lower modeling cost' without providing comparative evidence. The case study lacks comparison with existing action space reduction methods or baseline approaches. The paper only compares different action subsets within its own framework but does not evaluate against alternative techniques. Without baseline comparisons or ablation studies demonstrating performance improvements and cost reductions, this claim is unsubstantiated.

comments.title: Inconsistent computational complexity claim
comments.quote: The state-action mapping and transition probability are modeled using time-series auto-regression *[38]* after a PCA-based dimensionality reduction *[41]* with time complexity $\mathcal{O}(klogk)$, where $k$ is the number of principal components.
comments.explanation: The paper claims PCA has time complexity O(k log k), but standard PCA algorithms have complexity O(min(nk², kn²)) where n is the number of data points and k is the number of components. The O(k log k) complexity is incorrect unless the authors are using a very specific approximation algorithm not mentioned. This matters because the computational complexity claim is used to support the overall efficiency argument.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated search space reduction claim
comments.quote: Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4. That amounts to  $81.25\%$  reduction in the search space.
comments.explanation: The 81.25% reduction claim is mathematically incorrect. The original power set of 5 actions has 2⁵ = 32 possible subsets. Focusing on 6 training action sets (as shown in Table 4) represents a reduction of (32-6)/32 = 81.25%, but this calculation is misleading because the paper excludes subsets with cardinality less than 4. The actual search space after applying the cut-off cardinality of 4 would be C(5,4) + C(5,5) = 5 + 1 = 6 subsets, which is exactly what they use.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology for dispensable actions
comments.quote: However, the RL agent might perform sub-optimally without a disposable action point. By ranking disposable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards, the relative contributions of different training action points are estimated.
comments.explanation: There is a terminology inconsistency. The text establishes 'dispensable' as the technical term in the categorization {dispensable, indispensable}, but then uses 'disposable' in the ranking description. In a technical framework, precise terminology is essential for implementation and reproducibility. Replace 'disposable' with 'dispensable' in both instances to maintain consistent terminology throughout the framework description.

comments.title: Contradictory action set description
comments.quote: Figure 4 shows the RL loop action with three training actions:  $<\text{small } t3a, \text{ medium } t3a, \text{ large } t3a, \text{ xlarge } t3a, 2 \text{ xlarge } t3a>$ ,  $<\text{medium } t3a, \text{ large } t3a, \text{ xlarge } t3a, 2 \text{ xlarge } t3a>$ , and  $<\text{small } t3a, \text{ large } t3a, \text{ xlarge } t3a, 2 \text{ xlarge } t3a>$ .
comments.explanation: The text states Figure 4 shows 'three training actions' but then lists three training action sets, each containing 4-5 actions. This is confusing because it contradicts the terminology used throughout the paper where 'training actions' refer to individual EC2 instance types, not sets of them. The description should say 'three training action sets' to maintain consistency with the paper's terminology and Table 4.

comments.title: Missing algorithm reference
comments.quote: Using Algorithm ??, the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0.1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The text references 'Algorithm ??' which appears to be a placeholder or missing reference. This makes it impossible to verify how the step-size of 0.1 was determined. The missing algorithm number needs to be corrected to maintain the paper's reproducibility.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a Shapley-inspired framework for pruning and ranking reinforcement learning action spaces to improve training efficiency. It introduces a Monte Carlo-based approach to estimate action contributions and evaluates the method on a cloud resource tuning case study.

The paper addresses a practical and relevant problem in RL, specifically the challenge of high-dimensional or poorly defined action spaces. While the proposed methodology is intuitive, the empirical validation is limited to a single, small-scale cloud infrastructure task, which makes it difficult to assess the generalizability or scalability of the approach. The paper would benefit from more rigorous testing across standard RL benchmarks.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Empirical Breadth and Generalization: The methodology is evaluated exclusively on a single cloud resource tuning task with five action points. This is insufficient to support the claim that the approach is 'extensible to different domains' and 'agnostic of use cases.' Without experiments on standard RL benchmarks like Atari, MuJoCo, or classic control tasks, it is impossible to determine if the pruning strategy holds up under different environment dynamics or if the performance gains are specific to the chosen linear-ish cloud workload. The authors should include at least two diverse environments to demonstrate that the action ranking and cut-off logic are not artifacts of the specific case study.

- Computational Complexity and Scalability Concerns: Section 2.3 acknowledges that exact Shapley computation is exponential, but the proposed Monte Carlo approximation is not rigorously analyzed for convergence or error bounds. In high-dimensional action spaces, the number of samples required to achieve stable rankings may be prohibitively large, potentially negating the benefits of pruning. The paper needs to provide a complexity analysis or empirical evidence showing how the number of required simulations scales with the cardinality of the action space. Without this, the claim that the method enables 'high-performance yet cost-efficient' design remains speculative for larger action sets.

- Sensitivity to Hyperparameters and Training Instability: The methodology relies on a threshold parameter epsilon and a fixed number of iterations n to determine the cut-off cardinality and categorize actions. These parameters are likely highly sensitive to the underlying RL algorithm's hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, exploration schedule). If the agent fails to converge due to poor hyperparameter tuning, the ranking of action contributions will be fundamentally flawed. The paper should conduct an ablation study to demonstrate how sensitive the resulting rankings are to these thresholds and the underlying RL agent's performance.

- Ambiguity in 'Indispensable' Action Definition: Algorithm 2 defines an action as indispensable if the agent fails to achieve the goal upon its removal. This binary categorization is overly rigid and depends heavily on the specific reward threshold and the agent's capacity to learn. In many RL settings, an action might be redundant under one policy initialization but critical under another. The authors should clarify whether this classification is policy-dependent and discuss how it accounts for the stochastic nature of RL training, perhaps by repeating the evaluation across multiple random seeds.

- Absence of sensitivity analysis for the Monte Carlo approximation: The methodology relies on a Monte Carlo simulation to estimate Action Shapley values, yet the paper provides no evidence regarding the variance of these estimates or the sample size required for convergence. Without a discussion on the error bounds of the approximation, the validity of the resulting rankings is unclear, especially when action contributions are subtle. The authors should provide a plot showing the stability of the ranking list $\mathcal{L}$ as a function of the number of Monte Carlo iterations for the cloud resource tuning case study. This would quantify the trade-off between computational cost and the reliability of the pruning decisions.

- Lack of comparison to standard action pruning baselines: The paper claims to provide a general-purpose methodology for action space evaluation, but it fails to compare its results against simpler, established baselines like random action sampling or variance-based pruning. It is currently impossible to determine if the Shapley-inspired approach offers superior performance or if the observed 80% reduction in search space could be achieved with significantly lower overhead using heuristic methods. The authors should include a comparison against at least one naive baseline, such as pruning actions with low average reward over a short initial training phase, to justify the complexity of the proposed framework.

- Undefined behavior for continuous action spaces: The paper claims the methodology is agnostic to machine learning algorithms and extensible to different domains, yet the provided algorithms and case study are strictly limited to discrete action sets. In many real-world RL applications, such as robotics or continuous control, the action space is continuous or high-dimensional, rendering the power set approach and the discrete categorization in Algorithm 2 inapplicable. To support the claim of general-purpose utility, the authors should provide a theoretical extension or a concrete example of how the framework handles a discretized continuous action space, specifically addressing how the 'indispensable' classification would function when actions are sampled from a continuous range.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Expand the empirical evaluation to include at least two standard RL benchmarks (e.g., CartPole and a MuJoCo task) to demonstrate the method's generalizability.

- Provide a sensitivity analysis of the epsilon and n parameters to show how they affect the cut-off cardinality and action rankings.

- Include a discussion or experiment regarding the stability of the Action Shapley values across multiple random seeds for the agent.

- Clarify the computational cost trade-off by plotting the time spent on Shapley estimation versus the time saved during training.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of the optimal policy function
comments.quote: RL agent training involves recognizing an optimal policy function, $\pi^{*}:a\leftarrow s$, from a corpus of $\{\langle s_{i},a_{i}\rangle\}$ to maximize the long-term cumulative reward, $\sum R_
comments.explanation: The notation \pi^{*}:a\leftarrow s implies a mapping from action to state, which is the inverse of the standard definition of a policy \pi: S \to A. Furthermore, the expression \sum R_{a} is ambiguous as it lacks indices and does not clearly represent the expected return. Please rewrite this as \pi^{*}: s \to a and define the objective as \mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t R_t] to align with standard reinforcement learning terminology.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in cut-off cardinality assignment
comments.quote:
if all statuses for a given cardinality,  $k$ , are failures then
$|A|_{cutoff} \gets k + 1$
end if
en
comments.explanation: The algorithm iterates from k = |A| down to 1. If the condition 'all statuses for a given cardinality k are failures' is met, the algorithm assigns |A|_{cutoff} = k + 1. However, if the loop continues to smaller values of k, and those also result in failures, the assignment |A|_{cutoff} \gets k will overwrite the previous assignment. This logic identifies the smallest failing set size rather than the threshold for efficiency. The loop should break immediately after the assignment to ensure the largest cardinality that fails is captured.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of algorithm agnosticism
comments.quote: Additionally, the proposed methodology is completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms. Therefore, it is a general-purpose methodology extensible to different domains including distributed computing, network traffic control, healthcare, automatic locomotion, building management system, and industrial controls, and different machine learning algorithms such as PCA, Autoencoder, ARIMA, LSTM, Transformer, PID, SARSA, DQN, and many others.
comments.explanation: The claim that the methodology is 'completely agnostic' of machine learning algorithms is not supported by the technical content. The proposed approach relies on the specific structure of an RL agent's training loop to evaluate action importance via cumulative reward. It is unclear how this framework would be applied to non-RL algorithms like PCA or ARIMA, which do not inherently utilize an action space or a reward-based policy optimization process. Please remove the list of non-RL algorithms as the methodology is fundamentally tied to the RL paradigm.

comments.title: Inconsistent action set cardinality in text and table
comments.quote: The RL training action space is spanned by the power set drawn from the five pairs of EC2 configuration set points as shown in Table 2. Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results. Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4.
comments.explanation: The text states the analysis focuses on six training action sets, but Table 4 lists only six rows total, three of which are marked as 'none' for reward. If the power set of five actions has 2^5 - 1 = 31 possible non-empty subsets, focusing on only six specific sets requires justification. Please clarify how these six specific sets were selected from the 31 possible combinations and why the others were excluded.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'all necessary training action sets'
comments.quote: until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted.
comments.explanation: The algorithm refers to 'all necessary training action sets' for a given cardinality k, but does not define how these sets are selected or sampled from the power set P(A). If the algorithm is intended to be a Monte Carlo method, it must specify a sampling distribution or a stopping criterion for the 'repeat' loop. Without a defined number of samples or a convergence criterion for the estimation of the cumulative reward improvement, the 'until' condition is ill-defined.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim regarding prior work on Shapley in RL
comments.quote: To the best of our knowledge, Shapley value has not been used to quantify training action valuation in a reinforcement learning context.
comments.explanation: This statement is factually incorrect given the existence of recent literature, such as 'Action Shapley: A Training Data Selection Metric for High Performance World Models' (2024), which explicitly proposes using Action Shapley for selecting training actions in reinforcement learning. The authors should acknowledge these existing contributions to maintain an accurate representation of the research landscape.

comments.title: Inconsistency in terminology for dispensable actions
comments.quote: However, the RL agent might perform sub-optimally without a disposable action point. By ranking disposable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards, the relative contributions of different training action points are estimated.
comments.explanation: The text introduces the term 'dispensable' earlier in the paragraph to describe non-essential actions, but then switches to 'disposable' in this sentence. This inconsistency creates ambiguity for the reader regarding whether these are distinct categories or synonyms. Given that the algorithm defines the set as {dispensable, indispensable}, the term 'disposable' should be replaced with 'dispensable' throughout the section to maintain internal consistency.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a Shapley-inspired framework to evaluate and prune training action spaces in reinforcement learning, applying it to a cloud resource tuning case study. While the goal of reducing high-dimensional action spaces is relevant, the manuscript contains fundamental theoretical misalignments, algorithmic ambiguities, and insufficient empirical validation. The methodology conflates threshold-based ablation with cooperative game theory valuation, and the case study lacks the statistical rigor expected for RL research.

The paper tackles a practical problem in RL hyperparameter and action space design by attempting to quantify the marginal contribution of individual training actions. The application of cooperative game theory concepts to action selection is an interesting angle, and the cloud infrastructure case study provides a concrete testbed. However, the theoretical formulation deviates from established Shapley value properties, and the empirical evaluation does not meet standard benchmarks for statistical reliability or comparative analysis.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Misalignment with Standard Shapley Value Formulation: Equation 1 introduces an arbitrary constant $C$ without specifying its value or deriving it from the standard Shapley value definition. The canonical formula requires a combinatorial weighting factor of $\frac{|S|!(n-|S|-1)!}{n!}$, which ensures efficiency, symmetry, and additivity. By leaving $C$ undefined, the authors break the axiomatic guarantees that make Shapley values useful for attribution. This matters because the entire ranking and categorization logic in Section 2.2 rests on the assumption that the computed values represent equitable marginal contributions. The authors should either define $C$ explicitly to match the standard normalization or reframe the method as a heuristic approximation and justify the chosen weighting scheme.
